title: Spread and standard deviation
intro: The "Mama John's" chain has grown to two locations. The average salary at both is exactly 35k: at pizzeria A everyone earns roughly the same, at pizzeria B some earn much less, some much more. Same mean — completely different teams. To catch that difference you need a second characteristic: how far the values scatter around the center.

beats.text: Two pizzerias, both with an average salary of exactly 35k — the dashed line sits in the same spot in both rows. But the points of Pizzeria B are scattered noticeably wider than A's. To the right of each row is its σ, the standard deviation.
The mean of A and B is the same. The spread is not.
beats.predict: Press "shrink B" — the points of Pizzeria B pull toward the center. What do you think happens to Pizzeria B's mean and its σ?
beats.reveal: B's mean stays 35 (we shrink symmetrically toward the center), while σ drops — and Pizzeria B starts to look like the even A. Press "stretch B" and σ grows again. The mean stays put the whole time.

beats.text: That is what σ is for: A and B share one mean, but the teams differ. σ catches what the mean cannot see — how widely the values diverge around the center. A small σ — everyone hugs the middle; a large σ — the spread is wide.

σ is computed like this: for each value take its deviation from the mean, square it (so minuses don't cancel pluses), average them — that is the variance — and then take the square root to return to the original units. Because of the squaring, rare distant values weigh more and noticeably inflate σ.
σ has the same weakness as the mean: it is built around the mean, so one director at the edge inflates not only the mean but σ too.
beats.predict: Drag Pizzeria B's rightmost point far to the right. Which changes more — the mean or σ?
beats.reveal: Both grow, but σ jumps especially sharply: a distant value enters it squared. Most salaries did not change, yet the "typical spread" is already inflated by a single point — we'll deal with this when we get to percentiles.

beats.text: To tell whether the spread is large or not, it helps to compare σ with the mean. σ = 6 at a mean of 35 is a spread of about 17% — a fairly even team; σ = 14 at the same mean is almost 40% — salaries are all over the place. The ratio of σ to the mean is called the coefficient of variation: it lets you compare spread across quantities of different scale. Now let's pull together the main thing — what all this means in practice.

definitions.text: The difference between a single value and the mean. It shows how far that value sits from the center, and in which direction.
definitions.simple: how far a particular value has strayed from the mean.

definitions.text: The mean of the squared deviations from the mean. Squaring removes the signs and penalizes distant values more heavily. It is measured in squared original units, so it is usually not read directly — you take its square root instead.
definitions.simple: the average squared deviation from the mean.

definitions.text: A measure of how widely the values are scattered around their mean. It is the square root of the variance, so it is expressed in the same units as the data and reads as the "typical deviation from the mean".
definitions.simple: the typical distance from a point to the mean, in ordinary units.

definitions.text: The standard deviation divided by the mean — the spread as a fraction (or percentage) of the mean. It is independent of the units, so it lets you compare variability across quantities of different scale.
definitions.simple: the spread as a percentage of the mean — comparable across different quantities.

practice: Back to our two pizzerias. Both have the same average salary — 35k — but σ is about 6 at Pizzeria A and about 14 at Pizzeria B. The center coincides, yet life inside is completely different: at Pizzeria A salaries are predictable and stay close together, at Pizzeria B the gap between people is huge.

Hence the main takeaway of the lesson: the center is almost never looked at alone. "Mean plus spread" is the minimal honest pair that describes data: where it concentrates and how widely it diverges around that center.
realLife: Spread is everywhere that not only the magnitude but also the stability matters. Two couriers deliver in 30 minutes on average, but one stays within 25–35 while the other jumps from 10 to 60: same mean, different reliability — and σ shows it at once.
The same underlies the volatility of stocks (that is the σ of returns: the larger, the riskier) and quality control in manufacturing, where "beyond 3σ" is treated as a defect.
Next we'll look at what shapes data takes at all. There the mean and σ come into their own together: for a bell-shaped, "normal" distribution they almost fully define it.
assumptions: Standard deviation relies on the mean, so it inherits its weakness: one strong outlier inflates σ so much that the "typical spread" stops describing typical data. For skewed quantities (incomes, response times) it is more honest to look at spread through percentiles.
nextLabel: We have the center and the spread — but what shapes does the data itself take? → distributions and the bell
